topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red ink on the paper seemed to mock me. A C-minus. After weeks of late nights, fueled by lukewarm coffee and sheer stubbornness, this was the reward? I slammed the textbook shut, the sound echoing sharply in the quiet study room. My jaw tightened, a hard knot forming beneath my skin. I snatched the paper, my knuckles white as I gripped the edges. "This is absurd," I muttered, the words tasting like ash. I stared at the grading rubric, searching for some hidden flaw, some mistake, anything to justify this dismal result. My stomach churned with a hot, tight feeling. I wanted to rip the paper in half, to throw it across the room, but I forced myself to breathe, the frustration building into a slow, simmering heat behind my eyes.

[story 2]

I paced the length of my dorm room, the worn carpet muffling the frantic rhythm of my footsteps. Every time I stopped, I ran a hand through my already messy hair, feeling the familiar prickle of annoyance spread across my scalp. The number on the assignment sheet—a stark, unforgiving B-—felt like a physical blow. I leaned against the windowsill, staring out at the dreary campus quad, my shoulders slumping momentarily before they snapped back up with sharp tension. "They just don't get it," I hissed to the empty air, my voice low and vibrating with suppressed energy. I clenched my fists so tightly my nails dug crescent moons into my palms. I needed to find out *why*, and the sheer injustice of the outcome was making my chest feel incredibly constricted.

[story 3]

When the professor handed back the marked essays, I felt a sudden, cold wave wash over me, quickly replaced by a burning sensation in my throat. I didn't look at the score immediately. Instead, I meticulously smoothed the paper flat on my desk, my movements jerky and precise. My breath hitched when my eyes landed on the grade: a disappointing D. A slow, deliberate shake of my head followed. "Unbelievable," I whispered, the sound thin and brittle. I picked up my pen, not to correct the work, but just to twist it between my fingers until the plastic groaned. The silence in the room was heavy, charged with a furious, internal energy that made my whole body feel coiled and ready to spring.
